Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent even though I put in decent hours. I'd usually wait until I had a chunk of free time, then binge-watch lecture videos or listen to recordings while doing other things. Being an auditory learner, I found it easier to absorb information through listening rather than reading, so I'd often have lectures playing in the background. I rarely went to actual classes, but I made sure to catch up through the online materials when I could focus.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I have extracurricular commitments that sometimes took priority, so I'd have to cram studying into whatever gaps I could find. Some weeks I'd be really on top of things, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until a deadline loomed. I tried to be consistent but life just doesn't work that way for me.
Q3: Usually it was either an assignment deadline approaching or getting a notification that new content was posted. Sometimes my peer support group would mention something that made me realize I was behind, and that would push me to log in. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse around—there always had to be a specific reason pulling me there. The anxiety of missing something important was often my main motivator.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me—it always felt like something I had to consciously decide to do. The closest it got to routine was when I'd set aside Sunday evenings to catch up on everything I'd missed during the week. But even then, it felt more like damage control than an actual routine I looked forward to.
Q5: The video lectures and audio recordings were absolutely essential for me. I learn best by listening, so I'd often play these multiple times while commuting or doing chores. The peer interaction features were also helpful because my support group would discuss things there, and hearing different perspectives helped concepts click. Discussion posts weren't my thing to write, but reading others' questions sometimes clarified my own confusion.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and PDFs because I just don't absorb information well that way. Discussion forums I barely touched for posting—I only wrote like two posts the whole time. Some of the supplementary resources felt redundant when I'd already understood the concept from the videos. If something required me to sit and read for long periods, I'd put it off indefinitely.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to an assignment or exam. Also, if it was in a format that worked for my learning style—audio or video—I was much more likely to engage with it fully. When peers recommended something as helpful, that endorsement made me prioritize it too. Anything that felt like busywork without clear purpose got pushed to the bottom of my list.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. With my anxiety about keeping up with required work, optional stuff felt like extra pressure rather than enrichment. Required activities had this urgency attached that made me prioritize them, while optional ones I'd tell myself I'd get to 'later'—which usually meant never. It's not that I didn't want to learn more, I just had to triage constantly.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone during commutes or while exercising. I also had study sessions with peers from my support group where we'd discuss concepts without any of us being logged into anything. My notes were all handwritten because writing while listening helps me remember, but none of that shows up anywhere digital.
Q10: There were many times I'd be actively studying—listening to downloaded lectures, reviewing my handwritten notes, or discussing concepts with my peer group in person—and none of that would register on the LMS. Sometimes I'd spend hours going over material I'd already accessed once, just replaying it to reinforce understanding. The system would show I watched a video once, but I might have listened to the audio version five more times offline.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access materials once on the LMS, download what I could, and then work with those files offline for days or weeks. It was just more convenient than constantly logging in, especially when my internet wasn't always reliable. The LMS would show minimal activity, but I was actually engaging deeply with the content in my own way.
Q12: When my anxiety spiked, which happened more often than I'd like, it became really hard to engage even when I wanted to. There were weeks when the thought of logging in and seeing how much I'd missed just paralyzed me. My extracurricular commitments sometimes created time crunches that made everything feel overwhelming. But my resilience usually kicked in eventually, and I'd find a way to push through.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed, I'd log in but just kind of stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars meant I sometimes had to choose between activities, and coursework didn't always win. Confusion about course material would sometimes make me avoid the LMS entirely because I didn't know where to start. My peer support group helped me navigate these moments more than the LMS itself did.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other things or just zoning out. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials checking boxes mentally but not actually absorbing anything. It's like my body was going through the motions but my brain hadn't shown up. Those sessions felt productive in the moment but I'd realize later I retained nothing.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts because they stressed me out—putting my thoughts out there publicly triggered my anxiety. Sometimes I'd avoid starting an assignment because the scope felt overwhelming, even when I knew waiting would make it worse. The peer support helped me push through these avoidance patterns eventually. I knew what I was doing was counterproductive but anxiety doesn't really respond to logic.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire relationship with the LMS. I'd have bursts of intense activity right before things were due, then go quiet for stretches. My high assignment completion rate is honestly a testament to last-minute panic rather than good planning. The deadline pressure actually helped cut through my anxiety sometimes because the fear of failing outweighed the fear of engaging. It wasn't healthy but it worked.
Q17: When I got positive feedback, it gave me a boost that made me want to engage more. Constructive criticism sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me want to avoid the course for a bit. My peers' informal feedback in our support group actually influenced me more than official instructor feedback. Knowing someone thought I was on the right track helped quiet the anxious voice telling me I was failing.
Q18: The structure being heavily online actually worked for me in some ways because I could engage on my own schedule. But the lack of required attendance meant I rarely went to actual classes, which probably wasn't great for my learning. Having 14 online courses meant I was constantly juggling, and this course sometimes got lost in the shuffle. The flexibility was both a blessing and a curse for someone like me.
Q19: The availability of video and audio content made engagement much easier for my learning style. What made it harder was when important information was only in text format—I'd have to force myself through those. Clear deadlines helped because ambiguity stresses me out. The peer interaction features were good in theory, but the pressure to post publicly made me avoid them despite wanting to connect.
Q20: I think some students might be like me—logging in frequently to check on things, watching videos, downloading materials, but then doing the actual synthesis and assignment work offline. Or they could be anxiously checking the LMS without being able to focus on actually producing work. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity; sometimes it's just anxious scrolling or repeated checking without retention.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. My attendance was terrible and I wasn't super active in discussions, but I still managed a Merit grade because I was learning in ways the system couldn't see. Listening to lectures offline, studying with peers in person, making handwritten notes—all of that contributed to my performance but left no digital trace. The LMS only captures one narrow slice of learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access and completion, which is useful for identifying who's completely disengaged. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely—whether someone was actually thinking or just clicking through. They can't capture offline studying, peer discussions, or the mental work of processing information. They definitely can't measure anxiety, motivation, or whether someone is struggling silently while appearing active.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm genuinely curious and thinking about concepts even outside of study time. It's also feeling connected to the course through peers and feeling like my learning matters. You can be engaged without being visibly active, and you can be very active without being truly engaged.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support group carried me through this course emotionally and academically. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to lectures while walking or the anxiety attacks I had before logging in. It can't capture that I genuinely found some of this material interesting despite my sporadic visible engagement. My resilience in pushing through difficult moments, my growth in managing anxiety—none of that appears in any log.
